title: Articles définis et indéfinis
description: Definite (le, la, l', les) and indefinite (un, une, des) articles in French. Gender + elision rules.

cards.notes: Used before consonant-starting masculine nouns: **le chat**, **le livre**, **le garçon**.
Used before consonant-starting feminine nouns: **la chaise**, **la maison**, **la fille**.

cards.back: the (before a vowel or silent h)
cards.notes: Elided form of *le* / *la* before words starting with a vowel sound: **l'eau**, **l'enfant**, **l'homme** (silent h). NOT **le eau** — the elision is mandatory.
Same form for masculine and feminine plurals: **les chats**, **les chaises**, **les enfants**. Pronounce the s with a /z/ liaison before a vowel: *les_z_enfants*.
Indefinite version of *le*. **un chat** = a cat (one of many). Final n is nasal, not pronounced like English n.
Indefinite version of *la*. **une chaise** = a chair. Final e makes the n audible (unlike *un*).
Indefinite plural — used where English drops the article entirely. **J'ai des amis** = I have friends (not *I have some friends*). Same for both genders.
Don't confuse with **la livre** (the pound — weight or currency). Same spelling, different gender, different meaning.
Starts with a vowel — definite form is **l'eau**, not *la eau*.
Unusual: same spelling for masculine and feminine. The article tells the gender: **l'enfant** is elided; **un enfant** (a boy) vs **une enfant** (a girl).

steps.title: Why articles matter
steps.body:
# Articles in French

Every French noun has a **gender** — masculine or feminine — and the article you put in front of it changes shape to match. There's no neutral "the" / "a" the way English has.

- *le chat* — the cat
- *la chaise* — the chair
- *un livre* — a book
- *une maison* — a house

Get the article wrong and a French speaker will understand you, but it sounds off. Worse: a few words have different meanings depending on gender (*le livre* = the book, *la livre* = the pound). Learning the gender with the noun, not separately, is the standard advice.

This lesson covers both **definite** articles (the) and **indefinite** articles (a / an / some).

steps.title: Definite: le / la / les
steps.body:
## Definite articles

| English | French | When |
|---|---|---|
| the (m. sg.) | **le** | masculine, consonant start: *le chat*, *le livre* |
| the (f. sg.) | **la** | feminine, consonant start: *la chaise*, *la maison* |
| the (pl.) | **les** | any plural: *les chats*, *les chaises*, *les enfants* |

Definite means "the specific one we both know about." *Le chat* = the cat (a specific one), not just any cat.

The plural *les* doesn't care about gender — masculine and feminine plurals both take *les*.

exercise.prompt: Match each French noun with its correct definite article (le, la, or les).
Type the correct definite article for **chat** (the cat).
exercise.hint: Chat is masculine, singular, starts with a consonant.

steps.title: Elision: l' before vowels
steps.body:
## The elision rule

When the noun starts with a vowel sound (a, e, i, o, u, or silent h), **le** and **la** both shrink to **l'** — the apostrophe replaces the vowel of the article.

- *l'eau* — the water (NOT *la eau*)
- *l'enfant* — the child (NOT *le enfant* / *la enfant*)
- *l'homme* — the man (silent h, still elides)

This is mandatory in writing and in speech. There is no exception at A1 level.

*les* is NOT elided in writing — it stays *les*. But you'll hear a /z/ liaison: *les enfants* sounds like *lez-ahn-fahn*.

exercise.prompt: **eau** (water) is feminine. Which is the correct definite form?
exercise.hint: Eau starts with a vowel — the article elides.

exercise.prompt: Type the correct definite article (with apostrophe if needed) for **enfant** (child).
exercise.hint: Enfant starts with a vowel — elide.

steps.title: Indefinite: un / une / des
steps.body:
## Indefinite articles

| English | French | When |
|---|---|---|
| a / an (m.) | **un** | masculine singular: *un chat*, *un livre* |
| a / an (f.) | **une** | feminine singular: *une chaise*, *une maison* |
| some / -- (pl.) | **des** | any plural: *des chats*, *des chaises* |

Indefinite means "a generic one" or "some":

- *Je vois **un** chat.* — I see a cat.
- *J'achète **une** maison.* — I'm buying a house.
- *J'ai **des** amis ici.* — I have friends here.

Note the last one: English drops the article ("friends," not "some friends"), but French keeps **des**. Forgetting *des* in the plural is one of the most common A1 mistakes.

Elision applies the same way: *un* doesn't elide, but *une* keeps its vowel — there's no *un'arbre*.

exercise.prompt: Match each definite article with its indefinite counterpart.
Arrange the tiles to form: *I see a cat*.
exercise.hint: Same word order as English: subject, verb, article, noun.

exercise.prompt: Translate to French: *I have ___ friends here.* (Fill the article — French requires it even though English doesn't.)
exercise.hint: Plural, indefinite. The article that English silently drops.

exercise.prompt: Which sentence means *I see the children* (a specific group, definite)?
exercise.hint: Definite plural is *les*. The other options have grammar errors.

exercise.sentence: Je vois ___ chat dans le jardin.
exercise.hint: *un* is the masculine indefinite article — *the* would be definite (*le*); a feminine indefinite is *une*.
